Lecture 5: Solving Linear Equations and Null Space ​

Topics: §2.1–2.2 — Solving Ax=b via Cross-Filling, Null Space (Descriptive ↔ Constructive), Existence and Uniqueness Date: Mar 16–19, 2026

🎯 Motivation: Why We Need to Solve Equations ​

The Two Languages (Recap from Lecture 4) ​

In Lecture 4, we learned that every subspace can be described in two complementary ways:

LanguageHow it worksExample
DescriptiveList equations (constraints) members must satisfy{x:Ax=0}
ConstructiveProvide recipe to generate all membersspan{v1,v2,…}

Each language has strengths and weaknesses:

DescriptiveConstructive
Good at✅ Verification (checking membership)✅ Generation (producing members)
Bad at❌ Generation (hard to find examples)❌ Verification (hard to check membership)

Today's key insight: Solving equations helps both languages do what they're bad at!

Case 1: Column Space (Naturally Constructive) ​

The column space is naturally constructive:

Col(A)=span{columns of A}={Ax:x∈Rn}

Example

Example: Column space of meals

A=🥛☕🍵🍃002🍋001🫘024🐄101

Constructive description:

Col(A)=span{(0001),(0020),(2141)}

Generation (easy ✅): Want a vector in Col(A)? Just pick coefficients:

2(0001)+3(0020)=(0062)∈Col(A)

Verification (hard ❌): Is b=(42103) in Col(A)?

Can't tell by looking! Need to solve: Ax=b

  • If solution exists → b∈Col(A) ✓
  • If no solution → b∉Col(A) ✗

Solving equations gives constructive description the power to verify!

Case 2: Null Space (Naturally Descriptive) ​

The null space is naturally descriptive:

Null(A)={x∈Rn:Ax=0}

Example

Example: Null space of the same matrix

Using the same matrix A from above.

Descriptive definition:

Null(A)={x:(002001024101)x=0}

Verification (easy ✅): Is v=(01−2) in Null(A)?

Just compute:

A(01−2)=(−4−2−60)≠0

Not in null space! ✗ (Easy to check!)

Generation (hard ❌): The columns (🥛, ☕, 🍵) have redundancy — can you find it by staring?

Hard to see! Need to solve Ax=0 to get:

Null(A)=span{(02−1)}

What does this vector tell us? It records the dependence relation:

0⋅🥛+2⋅☕−1⋅🍵=0⇒2×☕=1×🍵

Ah! The 🍵 recipe uses exactly twice the ingredients of ☕ recipe. That's the redundancy!

Now generation is easy: t(02−1) for any t∈R (scalar multiples record the same relation).

Solving equations gives descriptive description the power to generate!

The Power of Solving Equations: Summary ​

Attention

Why Solving Equations is the Universal Tool

SubspaceNatural formNatural strengthNatural weaknessSolving equations provides
Col(A)Constructive (span)✅ Generation❌ VerificationSolve Ax=b → verify if b∈Col(A)
Null(A)Descriptive (equations)✅ Verification❌ GenerationSolve Ax=0 → get basis → generate all solutions

Two roles of solving equations:

  1. Language conversion:

    • Descriptive → Constructive: Solve Ax=0 to get basis for Null(A)
    • Constructive → Descriptive: Solve left equation xTA=0 to describe Col(A) as Null(B) (next lecture!)

  2. Complementing each language:

    • Constructive needs verification → Solve Ax=b (existence check)
    • Descriptive needs generation → Solve Ax=0 (find basis)

Today's focus: Develop a native cross-filling method to solve Ax=b, which simultaneously:

  • Gives constructive description of Null(A) (find basis)
  • Checks existence: Does b∈Col(A)?
  • Determines uniqueness: Is Null(A)={0}?
  • Finds all solutions when they exist

1. Null Space: Starting with the Descriptive Definition ​

1.1 Definition ​

Definition

Null Space (Kernel)

For a matrix A∈Rm×n, the null space is:

Null(A)={x∈Rn:Ax=0}

Geometric meaning: Null(A) stores all linear dependence relations among the columns of A.

Also called the kernel, denoted ker⁡(A).

Example

Example: Null space records redundancy in the menu

Consider a coffee shop menu matrix:

A=🥛 milk☕ coffee🍵 tea🍃 tea leaves002🍋 lemon001🫘 coffee beans024🐄 milk101

Question: Are there any redundant recipes? That is, is one meal just a scaled version of another?

Hard to see by staring! But if we find x∈Null(A), it will reveal the redundancy.

Suppose (we'll learn how to compute this soon) that:

x=(02−1)∈Null(A)

What does this vector tell us? Using the column view of Ax=0:

0⋅(0001)+2⋅(0020)+(−1)⋅(2141)=(0000)

Rearranging:

2⋅(0020)=1⋅(2141)

In coffee shop terms: 2×☕=1×🍵

Aha! The 🍵 tea recipe uses exactly twice the ingredients of the ☕ coffee recipe. That's the redundancy!

Remark

Key Insight

  • x=(02−1) doesn't mean "2 coffees minus 1 tea produces zero ingredients" (nonsense!)
  • It means: 🍵 = 2×☕ (a dependence relation)
  • Think of Null(A) as the "redundancy catalog"

Size of the catalog:

  • Null(A)={0} → no redundancy → columns linearly independent
  • dim⁡(Null(A))=k → there are k independent redundancy relations

:::

Proposition

Null Space is a Subspace

Null(A) is a subspace of Rn.

Quick proof: Verify the three axioms:

  1. 0∈Null(A) since A0=0 ✓
  2. If Ax1=0 and Ax2=0, then A(x1+x2)=0 ✓
  3. If Ax=0, then A(cx)=c(Ax)=0 ✓

1.2 The Problem: We Need Constructive Description ​

As we saw in the motivation, descriptive definitions are good at verification but bad at generation.

Example

Example 1.1: The verification-generation gap

A=(123246)

Look at the columns: a1=(12), a2=(24), a3=(36)

By eye, we can see redundancy: a2=2a1 and a3=3a1. So there should be dependence relations in Null(A) recording this!

Descriptive definition: Null(A)={x:Ax=0}

Verification (easy): Is v=(3−21) in Null(A)?

Check: Av=(1⋅3+2⋅(−2)+3⋅12⋅3+4⋅(−2)+6⋅1)=(24)≠0

Not in null space! ✗ (So this vector does not record a valid dependence relation.)

Generation (hard): Find a vector that does record the dependence relation "a2=2a1"...

We know 2a1−1a2+0a3=0, so (2−10) should be in Null(A). But how do we systematically find all such relations?

Random guessing doesn't work! We need a systematic method.

Attention

The Goal

Convert from descriptive (easy to verify, hard to generate):

Null(A)={x:Ax=0}

to constructive (easy to generate, basis):

Null(A)=span{v1,v2,…,vk}

Method: Solve the equation Ax=0 using cross-filling!

More generally, we'll develop a method for Ax=b which handles both null space and column space questions.

2. The Cross-Filling Method for Solving Ax=b ​

Now we develop the native method for solving linear equations using cross-filling. This method will:

  • Give constructive description of null space
  • Determine existence and uniqueness of solutions
  • Find all solutions when they exist

2.1 The Key Idea: Equations from Rank-One Pieces ​

Recall from Lecture 3: any matrix can be decomposed via cross-filling:

A=R1+R2+⋯+Rr

where each Ri is a rank-one matrix.

Key observation: For the augmented matrix (A∣b), we can similarly decompose:

(A∣b)=(R1∣b1)+(R2∣b2)+⋯+(Rr∣br)

Crucial insight: Since each (Ri∣bi) is rank-one, all its rows are scalar multiples of each other — they represent the same equation!

Example

Example 2.1: Rank-one matrix = one equation

Consider a rank-one augmented matrix:

(R1∣b1)=(246|8123|4369|12)=(213)(123|4)

All three rows represent the same equation:

  • Row 1: 2x1+4x2+6x3=8 (divide by 2) → x1+2x2+3x3=4
  • Row 2: 1x1+2x2+3x3=4
  • Row 3: 3x1+6x2+9x3=12 (divide by 3) → x1+2x2+3x3=4

Same equation! So one rank-one piece contributes one independent equation.

Attention

The Strategy

When we cross-fill (A∣b):

  1. Each rank-one piece (Ri∣bi) gives one equation
  2. Each successive piece has fewer variables (because cross-filling eliminates variables)
  3. We get a sequence of equations with decreasing number of variables
  4. This makes the system easy to solve by back-substitution!

Moreover: Each equation is a valid consequence of the original system.

2.2 Why Can We Solve the Simplified System? ​

The key question: We extract one equation from each rank-one piece. Why is solving these r equations equivalent to solving the original m equations?

Answer: Cross-filling gives (A∣b)=UV where:

  • U is m×r with linearly independent columns (Lecture 3 theorem)
  • V is r×(n+1) with rows from each rank-one piece

Since U's columns are independent, U can be left-cancelled:

UVz=0⟺Vz=0

Therefore the solution spaces are identical:

Solutions to (A∣b)=Solutions to V

We've reduced m equations to r equations (r≤m), and V's rows are exactly the simplified equations from cross-filling.

Remark

What is V?

V=(— first row of (R1∣b1) —— first row of (R2∣b2) —⋮— first row of (Rr∣br) —)

Each rank-one piece contributes one equation (all its rows are multiples of the first).

2.3 Observation: Variables Decrease, So Solve Backwards ​

After cross-filling (A∣b), we get:

(A∣b)=(R1∣b1)+(R2∣b2)+⋯+(Rr∣br)

Pause and observe: What pattern do these pieces have?

Remark

Key Observation: Progressive Variable Elimination

Each Ri corresponds to a pivot column ji. As cross-filling proceeds:

  • (R1∣b1) involves all n variables
  • (R2∣b2) involves n−1 variables (column j1 already eliminated)
  • (R3∣b3) involves n−2 variables (columns j1,j2 eliminated)
  • ⋮
  • (Rr∣br) involves n−r+1 variables (fewest!)

Structure of each equation:

  • Some columns are non-pivot → corresponding variables are free (choose any value)
  • One column is the new pivot → that variable can be solved in terms of free variables

Natural idea: Start from the end where things are simplest!

Attention

Backward Solving Strategy

Since (Rr∣br) has the fewest variables, solve backwards:

Step 1: Read (Rr∣br) (any non-zero row)

  • Solve for its pivot variable in terms of free variables

Step 2: Read (Rr−1∣br−1)

  • Solve for its pivot variable (can use free variables)

Step 3: Continue backwards through Rr−2,…,R2,R1

  • Each equation solves one more pivot variable
  • All pivot variables eventually expressed in terms of free variables

Result naturally appears:

  • General solution: Free variables are parameters, pivot variables are formulas
  • Particular solution: Set all free variables = 0
  • Null space basis: Set each free variable = 1 (one at a time), others = 0

Existence & uniqueness:

  • No solution if any (Ri∣bi) has Ri=0 but bi≠0 (contradiction 0=c)
  • Unique solution if no free variables (r=n)
  • Infinitely many solutions if free variables exist (r<n)

Let's see this in action.

2.4 Example: Backward Solving in Action ​

Example

Example 2.2: Solving a system using cross-filling

Solve:

(120124123613)(x1x2x3x4)=(51116)

Cross-fill the augmented matrix:

(A∣b)=[12015241211361316]

Pivot at (1,1):

(R1∣b1)=(123)(1201|5)=[12015240210360315]

Remainder after subtracting R1:

[000000010100101]

Pivot at (2,3):

(R2∣b2)=(011)(0010|1)=[000000010100101]

Done! rank(A∣b)=rank(A)=2 → solution exists ✓

Now observe the structure:

We have two rank-one pieces:

  • (R1∣b1): pivot at column 1 → involves variables x1,x2,x3,x4 (all 4)
  • (R2∣b2): pivot at column 3 → involves variable x3 only (1 variable!)

Pivot columns: 1, 3 → Pivot variables: x1,x3 Non-pivot columns: 2, 4 → Free variables: x2,x4 (choose freely!)

Backward solving: Start from R2 (simplest!)

From (R2∣b2) (read row 2):

x3=1

Easy! Variable x3 is just a constant.

From (R1∣b1) (read row 1):

x1+2x2+0x3+x4=5

Solve for the pivot x1:

x1=5−2x2−x4

Done! Both pivot variables expressed in terms of free variables.

General solution: Let x2=s and x4=t (free parameters)

x=(x1x2x3x4)=(5−2s−ts1t)=(5010)+s(−2100)+t(−1001)

Particular solution: Set s=t=0 → xp=(5010)

Null space basis: {(−2100),(−1001)} (one per free variable)

Two free variables → Infinitely many solutions (2-parameter family)

2.5 Reading Equations from Cross-Filling ​

Remark

Quick Method for Reading Solutions

Once you've cross-filled (A∣b), you can directly read the solution:

  1. Each rank-one piece (Ri∣bi) corresponds to a pivot column ji
  2. Read any non-zero row of (Ri∣bi) to get the equation
  3. Solve for xji in terms of non-pivot variables
  4. Back-substitute from later equations to earlier ones

Pattern: Equations naturally have fewer active variables as you progress through the cross-filling pieces.

3. Applications to Specific Cases ​

Now we apply the general method to specific important cases.

3.1 Application 1: Finding Null Space Basis (Constructive Description) ​

Proposition

Finding Null(A) via Cross-Filling

To find a basis for Null(A):

  1. Solve Ax=0 using cross-filling on (A∣0)
  2. Identify free variables: xj1,…,xjk
  3. For each free variable xji, create a basis vector:
    • Set xji=1
    • Set other free variables to 0
    • Solve for pivot variables
  4. The resulting k vectors form a basis for Null(A)

Result:

Null(A)=span{v1,…,vk}

We've converted from descriptive to constructive! ✓

Example

Example 3.1: Null space from Example 2.2

From Example 2.2, we found:

Null(A)=span{(−2100),(−1001)}

Descriptive → Constructive conversion complete!

Verification (easy with descriptive definition):

A(−2100)=−2(123)+1(246)=(000)$$✓$$A(−1001)=−1(123)+1(123)=(000)$$✓∗∗Dimension∗∗:$dim⁡(Null(A))=2$

Example

Example 3.2: Matrix with trivial null space

A=(102013001)

Cross-filling (A∣0):

All three columns are pivot columns → No free variables

General solution: Only x=0

Null(A)={0}

Dimension: dim⁡(Null(A))=0

3.2 Application 2: Existence Criterion ​

Theorem

Existence of Solutions

The equation Ax=b has a solution if and only if:

rank(A∣b)=rank(A)

Understanding via cross-filling:

Here's what actually happens when you cross-fill (A∣b):

Strategy: Choose all pivots in A's part (the first n columns), never in the b column.

Suppose rank(A)=r. Use these r pivots from A to decompose (A∣b):

(A∣b)=(R1∣b1)+⋯+(Rr∣br)+remainder

Now look at what's left in the b column:

Case 1: The b column is completely accounted for (remainder in b column = all zeros)

  • Then rank(A∣b)=r=rank(A) ✓
  • All equations from (Ri∣bi) are valid (no contradictions)
  • Solution exists ✓

Case 2: Something remains in the b column (remainder in b column ≠ zero)

  • Must take one more pivot in the last column to continue cross-filling
  • This gives a rank-one piece: (0∣c) where c≠0
  • Represents the equation: 0⋅x1+⋯+0⋅xn=c where c≠0
  • That's 0=c — contradiction! ✗
  • So rank(A∣b)=r+1=rank(A)+1
  • No solution ✗

Why can rank increase by at most 1? Because b is only one column! Can't add more than 1 to the rank.

Geometric interpretation:

b∈Col(A)⟺rank(A∣b)=rank(A)

Adding b as a column doesn't increase rank ⟺ b is already in the span of A's columns.

3.3 Application 3: Uniqueness Criterion ​

Theorem

Uniqueness of Solutions

If Ax=b has a solution, the solution is unique if and only if:

Null(A)={0}

Equivalently: No free variables (all columns are pivot columns).

Also equivalently: rank(A)=n (number of columns).

Proof:

Suppose x1 and x2 are both solutions. Then:

Ax1=bandAx2=b

Subtracting:

A(x1−x2)=0

So x1−x2∈Null(A).

  • If Null(A)={0}, then x1−x2=0, i.e., x1=x2 → Unique ✓
  • If Null(A)≠{0}, take any v∈Null(A) with v≠0. Then x1+v is also a solution → Not unique ✗

3.4 Application 4: Structure of Solution Set ​

Theorem

General Solution = Particular + Null Space

If Ax=b has a solution, then all solutions have the form:

x=xp+xn

where:

  • xp is any particular solution (one specific solution)
  • xn∈Null(A) is arbitrary

Proof:

(⇒) Suppose x is a solution. Pick any particular solution xp. Let xn=x−xp.

Then:

Axn=A(x−xp)=Ax−Axp=b−b=0

So xn∈Null(A) and x=xp+xn ✓

(⇐) Conversely, if x=xp+xn where Axp=b and xn∈Null(A):

Ax=A(xp+xn)=Axp+Axn=b+0=b$$✓

Geometric picture:

The solution set is a translated subspace (affine subspace):

  • Start at xp (particular solution)
  • Add any vector from Null(A) (a subspace through origin)
  • Get a parallel copy of Null(A) shifted to xp
     Solution set = x_p + Null(A)

     ╱──────────────╲
    ╱  Null(A)      ╲   (subspace through origin)
   ╱                 ╲
  •───────────────────•
  0

          ↓ shift by x_p

     ╱──────────────╲
    ╱                ╲   (affine subspace through x_p)
   ╱        x_p       ╲
  •───────────────────•

4. Dimension Formula: Rank-Nullity Theorem (Preview) ​

From the cross-filling method, we observed:

Proposition

Counting Pivot and Free Variables

When cross-filling an m×n matrix A:

  • Number of pivot columns = rank(A)
  • Number of free variables = dim⁡(Null(A))
  • Total columns = n

Therefore:

rank(A)+dim⁡(Null(A))=n

This is called the Rank-Nullity Theorem.

Proof idea: Every column is either a pivot column or a non-pivot column (contributing to null space basis), so they sum to the total number of columns.

We'll prove this more rigorously in the next lecture using the four fundamental subspaces.

Example

Example 4.1: Verifying rank-nullity

From Example 2.2:

  • A is 3×4
  • rank(A)=2 (two pivot columns)
  • dim⁡(Null(A))=2 (two free variables)
  • Check: 2+2=4 ✓

5. Null Space and Row Space: Orthogonality ​

5.1 The Natural Orthogonality ​

Theorem

Null Space is Perpendicular to Row Space

Null(A)⊥Row(A)

More precisely: Every vector in Null(A) is orthogonal to every vector in Row(A).

Proof:

Let x∈Null(A) and let ri be the i-th row of A.

By definition of null space: Ax=0

This means:

(—r1——r2—⋮—rm—)x=(00⋮0)

The i-th component gives: ri⋅x=0

So x is orthogonal to every row of A!

Since Row(A)=span{r1,…,rm}, and x is orthogonal to each ri, it's orthogonal to all linear combinations of them.

Therefore x⊥Row(A) ✓

Physical interpretation:

If A represents "meals → ingredients", then:

  • Row space: All possible ingredient combinations that can result from meals
  • Null space: Meal combinations that produce zero ingredients

These are perpendicular: A meal combination producing zero ingredients has nothing in common with actual ingredient requirements!

5.2 Geometric Picture ​

For an m×n matrix A:

Input space ℝⁿ splits into two perpendicular subspaces:

    Row(Aᵀ) = Col(A) ⊕ Null(A)

    ℝⁿ = Row(Aᵀ) ⊥ Null(A)
         ↑           ↑
    dim = rank(A)  dim = n - rank(A)

This is the foundation of the four fundamental subspaces, which we'll explore in detail next week!

6. Summary: The Complete Picture ​

::: success What We Achieved Today

1. Null Space:

  • Descriptive definition: {x:Ax=0}
  • Constructive description: span{v1,…,vk} (basis from solving)
  • Conversion: Descriptive → Constructive requires solving equations

2. Cross-Filling Method for Ax=b:

  • Each rank-one piece = one equation
  • Equations are valid (derived from original system)
  • Variables decrease with each piece
  • Natural solution by back-substitution

3. Existence: rank(A∣b)=rank(A) ⟺ solution exists

4. Uniqueness: Null(A)={0} ⟺ solution unique

5. General Solution: x=xp+xn where xn∈Null(A)

6. Rank-Nullity: rank(A)+dim⁡(Null(A))=n

7. Orthogonality: Null(A)⊥Row(A) :::

TIP

Conceptual Elevation

From Lecture 4: Column space shows what we can make (constructive)

From Lecture 5: Null space shows what makes zero (descriptive ↔ constructive via solving)

Together: These dual perspectives give complete understanding of linear systems!

  • Existence (can we reach b?) → Column space question
  • Uniqueness (one solution or many?) → Null space question
  • All solutions → Structure via particular + null space

Next lecture: Four fundamental subspaces and the complete orthogonal decomposition!

7. Comparison: Cross-Filling vs. Traditional Methods ​

Remark

How This Differs from Textbook Methods

Traditional approach (Gaussian elimination):

  1. Use row operations to get RREF (reduced row echelon form)
  2. Read solutions from the simplified form
  3. Focus: Algorithmic procedure

Our cross-filling approach:

  1. Decompose augmented matrix into rank-one pieces
  2. Each piece gives one equation with decreasing variables
  3. Focus: Understanding structure (why it works, what each piece means)

Advantages of cross-filling:

  • Conceptual clarity: See why each step is valid
  • Natural connection: Same method for rank, factorization, solving, null space
  • Geometric insight: Rank-one pieces have clear meaning
  • Flexibility: Can choose pivots strategically for different purposes

Both give the same answer! Cross-filling is the conceptual lens for understanding; RREF is a computational optimization.

Exercises ​

Conceptual Understanding ​

  1. Descriptive vs. Constructive:

    (a) Explain why the null space definition {x:Ax=0} is "descriptive"

    (b) Why is finding a basis for null space a "constructive" description?

    (c) What computational step converts descriptive → constructive?

  2. Validity of equations: Explain in your own words why each equation obtained from a rank-one piece (Ri∣bi) during cross-filling is a valid consequence of the original system.

Computing Null Space ​

  1. Find null space basis: For each matrix, use cross-filling to find a basis for Null(A):

    (a) A=(123246)

    (b) A=(102001300001)

    (c) A=(111123135)

  2. Verify rank-nullity: For each matrix in Exercise 3, verify:

    rank(A)+dim⁡(Null(A))=n

Solving Linear Systems ​

  1. Complete solution via cross-filling: Solve using cross-filling or show no solution exists:

    (a) (1224)(xy)=(36)

    (b) (1224)(xy)=(35)

    (c) (101011112)(xyz)=(235)

  2. Existence and uniqueness: For each system in Exercise 5:

    (a) Check the rank condition for existence

    (b) Determine if the solution (when it exists) is unique

    (c) If not unique, describe the solution set as particular + null space

Orthogonality ​

  1. Null space perpendicular to rows:

    (a) For A=(123456), find a basis for Null(A)

    (b) Verify that each basis vector is orthogonal to both rows of A

    (c) Express this geometrically: what is Null(A) in R3?

  2. Dimension formula: An m×n matrix has rank(A)=r.

    (a) What is dim⁡(Null(A))?

    (b) If m<n, can the null space be trivial? Why or why not?

    (c) If m>n and A has full rank, what is Null(A)?

Challenge Problems ​

  1. Structure of solution sets:

    (a) Prove that if Ax=b has solutions, the solution set is never a subspace (unless b=0)

    (b) What geometric object is the solution set? (Hint: affine subspace)

  2. Null space of products:

    (a) Prove: Null(B)⊆Null(AB)

    (b) Find an example where the inclusion is strict

    (c) When does equality hold?

  3. Cross-filling vs. RREF:

    (a) Apply both cross-filling and traditional row reduction to solve: $$\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 & 1 \ 2 & 4 & 3 \ 3 & 6 & 4 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} x \ y \ z \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 \ 7 \ 10 \end{pmatrix}$$

    (b) Compare the intermediate steps. How are the rank-one pieces related to elementary row operations?

  4. Dimension and orthogonality:

    (a) Prove using rank-nullity that dim⁡(Row(A))+dim⁡(Null(A))=n

    (b) Combine with orthogonality to conclude: Rn=Row(A)⊕Null(A) (orthogonal direct sum)

This lecture established the native cross-filling method for solving linear equations, revealing the deep connection between descriptive and constructive descriptions of subspaces. Next: the four fundamental subspaces and complete orthogonal structure!
